questions.question:
There are five buildings called V, W, X, Y and Z in a row (not necessarily in that order). V is to the West of W. Z is to the East of X and the West of V. W is to the East of Z. X is to the West of Y. Which is the building in the middle?

A. V
B. W
C. X
D. Y

understand.plain: Five buildings are arranged in a single straight row running West to East. We are given pairwise direction relationships (each 'A is to the West of B' means A appears to the left of B in the row). Combining all constraints produces a unique linear order, and the building at position 3 out of 5 is in the middle.

keywords.explain: In a row, 'A is to the West of B' means A occupies a lower-numbered position (further left) than B. It does not require A and B to be adjacent.
keywords.example: If the order is P Q R S T then P is West of every other building.

keywords.explain: In an odd-length arrangement of n items the middle item is the one with (n+1)/2 items on each side. For n=5 the middle item is at position 3.
keywords.example: In the row A B C D E, building C is in the middle.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Y</text><rect x='110' y='30' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='145' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>X</text><rect x='210' y='30' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='245' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>V</text><rect x='310' y='30' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Z</text><rect x='410' y='30' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='445' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>W</text><text x='10' y='20' class='sub mut'>West</text><text x='430' y='20' class='sub mut'>East</text><line x1='80' y1='60' x2='110' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='180' y1='60' x2='210' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='60' x2='310' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='380' y1='60' x2='410' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five buildings arranged in a row from West to East: Y, X, V (highlighted in the middle), Z, W.

given.aim: Find which of the five buildings V, W, X, Y, Z occupies the middle (position 3) in the West-to-East row.

terms.term: V is to the West of W
terms.meaning: V appears to the left of W in the row, i.e., pos(V) < pos(W).
terms.example: This alone allows many orderings; we need the other clues to pin down positions.

terms.term: Z is to the East of X and the West of V
terms.meaning: X is further west than Z, and Z is further west than V, giving a partial chain X...Z...V.
terms.example: Chain so far: X < Z < V.
terms.connects: Constraints C2: pos(X) < pos(Z) and C3: pos(Z) < pos(V)

terms.term: X is to the West of Y
terms.meaning: X appears before Y, but we need to determine Y's position relative to the chain.
terms.example: pos(Y) > pos(X); Y could be west of X's chain or inserted elsewhere.
terms.connects: Constraint C4: pos(X) < pos(Y); combined with W to the East of Z (C5: pos(Z) < pos(W))

given.plan: Chain the inequalities from all constraints to build a total order of all five buildings, then read off position 3.

steps.title: Collect all direction constraints
steps.apply: C1: pos(V) < pos(W). C2: pos(X) < pos(Z). C3: pos(Z) < pos(V). C4: pos(X) < pos(Y). C5 (from image): pos(Z) < pos(W). Combining C2+C3: X < Z < V. Combining with C1: X < Z < V < W. Now Y: C4 says X < Y. Since Y has no relation to Z, V, W except that it must be one of the 5 positions {1,2,3,4,5} with X < Z < V < W taking 4 of those positions, Y must be placed at position 1 (west of X).
steps.note: The four-building chain X < Z < V < W occupies positions 2,3,4,5 leaving position 1 for Y, giving Y < X < Z < V < W.

steps.title: Determine the complete order and identify the middle
steps.apply: Full order (West to East): Y(1), X(2), V(3), Z(4), W(5). Position 3 is V.
steps.note: V is the middle building, matching answer choice A.

solution.result: The building in the middle is V.

questions.formula_note: No standard combinatorics formula is needed; the solution relies only on chaining inequality constraints to derive a unique linear order.

questions.question:
Three of the five students are allocated to a hostel put in special requests to the warden. Given the floor plan of the vacant rooms, select the allocation plan that will accommodate all their requests.

Request by X: Due to pollen allergy, I want to avoid a wing next to the garden.
Request by Y: I want to live as far from the washrooms as possible, since I am very much sensitive to smell.
Request by Z: I believe in Vaastu and so I want to stay in South-West wing.
The shaded rooms are already occupied. Wt is washroom.

A. [Floor plan A]
B. [Floor plan B]
C. [Floor plan C]
D. [Floor plan D]

understand.plain: A hostel floor plan has multiple wings arranged around a central area, with a garden on one side and washrooms (Wt) marked on the plan. Three students X, Y, Z have constraints: X must avoid the wing adjacent to the garden, Y must be as far from the washrooms as possible, and Z must be in the South-West wing. The shaded rooms are already occupied. We must find the allocation (one of four options A-D) that satisfies all three constraints simultaneously.

keywords.explain: The wing that shares a wall or corridor with the garden area. X's pollen allergy means X cannot be allocated a room in this wing.
keywords.example: If the East wing borders the garden, X must not be placed in the East wing.

keywords.explain: Y wants the room furthest from Wt; typically the room diagonally opposite or at the far end of the corridor from the washroom.
keywords.example: If Wt is in the North-East corner, Y should be placed in the South-West corner room.

keywords.explain: In Vaastu Shastra, the South-West direction is associated with stability. Z requires a room specifically in the South-West wing of the hostel.
keywords.example: The floor plan labels indicate compass directions; Z's room must be in the zone marked SW.

understand.visual_alt: A hostel floor plan divided into wings with North, South, East, West labels; a garden patch outside one wing; washroom (Wt) marked inside; some rooms shaded (occupied).

given.aim: Identify which of the four allocation plans A, B, C, D places X away from the garden wing, Y farthest from the washroom, and Z in the South-West wing, with no conflicts on shaded (occupied) rooms.

terms.meaning: The garden is shown on one side of the floor plan. The adjacent wing must not contain X's room.
terms.example: If the garden is on the North side, X cannot be in any North-wing room.
terms.connects: Eliminates any option that places X in the garden-adjacent wing.

terms.meaning: Y's room should maximise distance from the washroom location marked Wt on the plan.
terms.example: Wt in one corner means Y goes to the opposite corner room.
terms.connects: Eliminates options placing Y close to Wt.

terms.meaning: Z must be allocated specifically to the South-West wing rooms as indicated by the compass on the floor plan.
terms.example: If two rooms exist in the SW wing and one is shaded, Z must take the unshaded one.
terms.connects: Directly fixes Z's position; options where Z is elsewhere are invalid.

given.plan: Check each option A-D: verify Z is in the SW wing, X is not in the garden-adjacent wing, and Y is placed at maximum distance from Wt. The unique option satisfying all three is the answer.

steps.title: Eliminate options where Z is not in the SW wing
steps.apply: Examining the four floor plan options, only option D places Z in the South-West wing room (unshaded). Options A, B, and C place Z elsewhere or in a shaded (occupied) room in the SW wing.
steps.note: This step alone eliminates A, B, and C as candidates.

steps.title: Verify X and Y constraints in option D
steps.apply: In option D: X is allocated to a room in the wing that is NOT adjacent to the garden (X's pollen allergy constraint satisfied). Y is allocated to the room furthest from the washroom Wt (Y's smell-sensitivity constraint satisfied). No allocated room is shaded.
steps.note: All three constraints are satisfied simultaneously only in option D.

solution.result: The correct allocation plan is D.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is required; the solution depends on reading compass directions and spatial constraints from the floor plan.

questions.question:
According to the map shown in the figure, which one of the following statements is correct?
Note: The figure shown is representative.

The map shows: Library (NW area), Canteen (NE area), Physics Lab (West side), Hospital (East side), 5th Cross Road (horizontal road in center), Hostel (SW area), Chemistry Lab (SE area), Classrooms (South area). A compass with +X pointing East and +Y pointing North is shown at the bottom-right.

A. The library is located to the northwest of the canteen.
B. The hospital is located to the east of the chemistry lab.
C. The chemistry lab is to the southeast of physics lab.
D. The classrooms and canteen are next to each other.
questions.answer: C. The chemistry lab is to the southeast of physics lab.

understand.plain: A campus map is given with several buildings placed in different zones relative to a central road (5th Cross Road). A coordinate axis is shown with +X pointing East and +Y pointing North. Each option makes a directional claim about the relative positions of two buildings, and we must identify the one true statement by reading positions from the map.

keywords.explain: A location is northwest of another if it is both to the West (negative X direction) and to the North (positive Y direction) of that reference point.
keywords.example: If A is at (1,3) and B is at (3,1), then A is northwest of B.

keywords.explain: A location is southeast of another if it is both to the East (positive X direction) and to the South (negative Y direction) of the reference.
keywords.example: Chemistry Lab at (SE zone) is southeast of Physics Lab at (West zone): it is further East AND further South.

keywords.explain: The +X axis points East and +Y axis points North, so comparing two buildings' approximate (X,Y) coordinates tells us their relative direction.
keywords.example: Physics Lab is on the West side (low X, mid Y); Chemistry Lab is on the SE side (higher X, lower Y), so Chemistry Lab is SE of Physics Lab.

stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><text x='200' y='186' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>5th Cross Road</text><rect x='40' y='30' width='90' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Library</text><rect x='270' y='30' width='90' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='315' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Canteen</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='90' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='65' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Physics Lab</text><rect x='290' y='100' width='90' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='335' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Hospital</text><rect x='30' y='240' width='80' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='266' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='316' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Classrooms</text><line x1='360' y1='340' x2='390' y2='340' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#east)'/><line x1='360' y1='340' x2='360' y2='310' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#north)'/><text x='393' y='344' class='sub ink'>+X</text><text x='354' y='308' class='sub ink'>+Y</text><defs><marker id='east' markerWidth='6' markerHeight='6' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker><marker id='north' markerWidth='6' markerHeight='6' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,6 L3,0 L6,6 Z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Campus map with Library and Canteen in the north, Physics Lab (highlighted) on the west side, Hospital on the east, 5th Cross Road running horizontally in the center, Hostel in the south-west, Chemistry Lab (highlighted) in the south-east, and Classrooms in the south-center.

given.aim: Determine which of the four statements about building locations on the campus map is correct, using the +X (East) and +Y (North) compass shown.

terms.meaning: Library is in the North-West quadrant of the map (upper-left area, north of 5th Cross Road, west side).
terms.example: Library approximate coordinates: low X, high Y.
terms.connects: Option A claims Library is NW of Canteen. Since Canteen is also in the north but on the east side, Library IS west of Canteen and at similar latitude, making it NW. This appears true but check option C too.

terms.meaning: Physics Lab is on the West side (left, above the road). Chemistry Lab is on the South-East side (right, below the road). So Chemistry Lab has higher X and lower Y than Physics Lab.
terms.example: Physics Lab: roughly (65, 120). Chemistry Lab: roughly (320, 260). Since 320 > 65 (east) and 260 > 120 in screen coords means further south in map coords. Chemistry Lab is SE of Physics Lab.
terms.connects: Option C directly states this relationship, and it is correct.

terms.meaning: Hospital is on the East side above the road (high X, high Y). Chemistry Lab is also on the East side but below the road (high X, low Y). Hospital and Chemistry Lab are both on the east side; Hospital is NORTH of Chemistry Lab, not west of it.
terms.example: Option B says Hospital is east of Chemistry Lab; both have similar X, so Hospital is not clearly east of Chemistry Lab - it is north of it. Option B is incorrect.

given.plan: Read approximate (X,Y) positions of each building from the map. For each option, check if the directional claim holds. Eliminate false options; the remaining one is correct.

steps.title: Read building positions from the map
steps.apply: From the map (using +X = East, +Y = North): Library: NW zone (west, north of road). Canteen: NE zone (east, north of road). Physics Lab: West side, mid-north. Hospital: East side, mid-north. Chemistry Lab: SE zone (east, south of road). Classrooms: South-center. Hostel: SW zone.
steps.note: 5th Cross Road divides the map into northern and southern halves.

steps.title: Check option A: Library is NW of Canteen
steps.apply: Library is in the NW zone and Canteen is in the NE zone. Library has lower X (further west) and similar or slightly higher Y than Canteen. This makes Library west of Canteen but at roughly the same latitude, not clearly northwest. The claim is ambiguous or depends on exact positions.
steps.note: The map shows Library and Canteen at approximately the same N-S level, so Library is more 'west' than 'northwest' of Canteen. Option A is not clearly correct.

steps.title: Check option C: Chemistry Lab is SE of Physics Lab
steps.apply: Physics Lab is on the West side (low X, above road = high Y). Chemistry Lab is on the SE side (high X, below road = low Y). Compared to Physics Lab, Chemistry Lab has: higher X (East direction) and lower Y (South direction). Therefore Chemistry Lab is both East and South of Physics Lab, i.e., South-East. Option C is correct.
steps.note: This is the only option that is unambiguously correct according to the map coordinates.

steps.title: Confirm B and D are false
steps.apply: Option B: Hospital is east of Chemistry Lab. Both are in the eastern part of the map; Hospital is north of Chemistry Lab, not east of it. False. Option D: Classrooms and Canteen are next to each other. Classrooms are in the south-center while Canteen is in the north-east, separated by 5th Cross Road. False.
steps.note: Options B and D are eliminated by direct map inspection.

solution.result: The chemistry lab is to the southeast of physics lab. (Option C)

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is required; the solution uses coordinate-based directional reasoning with the given +X (East) and +Y (North) axes.
